title: The field of a charged disk: between a point and a sheet
scenario: A disk of radius $R = 0.3$ m carries a uniform surface charge density $\sigma = 100$ nC/m². We want the electric field a distance $z = 0.1$ m out along its axis. Algebra hands you two extremes — a *point* charge $kQ/z^2$ and an *infinite* sheet $\sigma/2\varepsilon_0$ — but a real disk is neither, and there is no algebra formula for one. Calculus is the only way in: slice the disk into concentric rings $dq = \sigma\,2\pi r\,dr$, each contributing $k\,dq\,z/(z^2+r^2)^{3/2}$ on the axis, and integrate. The shaded area under those ring contributions is the field $E = \tfrac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}(1 - z/\sqrt{z^2+R^2})$ — which collapses to the point charge far away and to the infinite sheet for a huge disk.

assumptions.claim: A flat disk with uniform surface charge density $\sigma$, and the field evaluated on its central axis — so by symmetry the off-axis components of each ring cancel and only the axial field survives.

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (two extremes, nothing between)
steps.prose: Algebra gives the field of a *point* charge and of an *infinite* sheet — but a real disk is neither. Its charge is spread over a finite area, every ring a different distance from the axis point, and there is no algebra formula for that. This is where algebra runs out.

point_charge_approx.label: Point-charge approx $kQ/z^2$ (exact only far away; overestimates up close)

steps.label: Slice the disk into rings and add their on-axis fields
steps.prose: Cut the disk into concentric rings of radius $r$ and width $dr$, each carrying charge $dq = \sigma\,2\pi r\,dr$. By symmetry only the axial part of each ring's field survives, $k\,dq\,z/(z^2+r^2)^{3/2}$. Summing over the rings is an integral — the only way to handle a continuous distribution.

steps.label: The total field is the area under the ring contributions
steps.prose: Integrating the ring contributions gives the disk's field — the shaded area under $dE/dr$. The slope of the accumulated-field curve is the contribution at the cursor; the area under the contributions is the field. With $2\pi k = 1/2\varepsilon_0$ this is the familiar $\tfrac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}(1 - z/\sqrt{z^2+R^2})$.

steps.label: It bridges both algebra limits
steps.prose: Far from the disk ($z \gg R$) the field collapses to the point charge $kQ/z^2$ with $Q = \sigma\pi R^2$ — the whole disk seen from afar is just a point. For a very large disk ($R \to \infty$) it becomes the infinite sheet $\sigma/2\varepsilon_0$, the same at every distance. Drag the cursor to sweep how much of the disk is included, and $\sigma$ to scale the charge.

proof.heading: A finite disk has no algebra formula — it sits between the point charge and the infinite sheet. Calculus integrates the rings, and the area under them is the field.
proof.detail: check $dE/dr = 2\pi k\sigma z\,r/(z^2+r^2)^{3/2}$; check $E = \int_0^r f\,dr'$; far limit $kQ/z^2$ ($z\to\infty$); large-disk limit $\sigma/2\varepsilon_0$ ($R\to\infty$)

checks.claim: The accumulated field's slope is the ring contribution: $\dfrac{dE}{dr} = \dfrac{2\pi k\sigma z\,r}{(z^2+r^2)^{3/2}}$.
The field is the area under the ring contributions: $E(r) = \int_0^r \dfrac{2\pi k\sigma z\,r'}{(z^2+r'^2)^{3/2}}\,dr'$.
Far away the disk looks like a point charge: $E \to kQ/z^2$ with $Q = \sigma\pi R^2$ as $z \to \infty$.
A very large disk is an infinite sheet: $E \to \sigma/2\varepsilon_0 = 2\pi k\sigma$, independent of $z$, as $R \to \infty$.

misconception.claim: A charged disk is basically a flat sheet, so its field is just $\sigma/2\varepsilon_0$ — or, if you prefer, lump all its charge at the centre and use $kQ/z^2$.
misconception.refuted_by: Both are *limits*, not the answer. $\sigma/2\varepsilon_0$ is the field of an *infinite* sheet (or the disk in the limit $z \to 0$); for a finite disk at finite distance it is an overestimate, because the missing charge beyond radius $R$ would only have added more field. $kQ/z^2$ is the *far-field* limit ($z \gg R$); up close it overestimates badly, because the inverse-square point model piles all the charge at one distance instead of spreading it from $r=0$ out to $R$. The true field is the integral $E = \tfrac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}(1 - z/\sqrt{z^2+R^2})$, which lies below both extremes and only matches one of them in its own regime. Drag the cursor to sweep how much of the disk is included; the area under the ring-contribution curve is the field.

practice.asks: the on-axis field of the charged disk
practice.prompt: A disk of radius $R = 0.3$ m carries surface charge density $\sigma = 100$ nC/m². What is the electric field a distance $z = 0.1$ m out along its axis?

choices.misconception: Used the infinite-sheet field $\sigma/2\varepsilon_0 = 2\pi k\sigma$. That is the limit of an unbounded disk; a finite disk produces less, by the factor $(1 - z/\sqrt{z^2+R^2})$.

Lumped all the charge $Q = \sigma\pi R^2$ into a point and used $kQ/z^2$. That is only valid far away ($z \gg R$); this close it overestimates, because the charge is actually spread from the axis out to radius $R$.

algebra_steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (two extremes, nothing between)
algebra_steps.prose: Algebra gives the field of a *point* charge and of an *infinite* sheet — but a real disk is neither. Its charge is spread over a finite area, every ring a different distance from the axis point, and there is no algebra formula for that. This is where algebra runs out.
